column1: #FORMAT=WebAnno TSV 3.3
#T_SP=custom.Span|label
#Text=THE PRESERVATION OF FOOD.
#Text=—All organic foods tend rapidly to decompose
#Text=and putrefy.
#Text=Putrefaction only occurs when a warm and moist substance
#Text=is exposed to the air.
#Text=The problem of preserving any food, therefore,
#Text=may be solved (1) by keeping it at a very low temperature, (2) by
#Text=desiccating it, or (3) by boiling or steaming it so as to destroy any
#Text=microbes in the food which would otherwise start putrefaction, and then
#Text=fastening it in an air-tight case.
#Text=Milk is commonly preserved as condensed milk, and in this condition
#Text=is very valuable.
#Text=A pure condensed milk is now supplied, prepared
#Text=without the addition of sugar or any antiseptic, but in which, as in
#Text=other condensed milks, all disease-producing or decomposition-producing
#Text=microbes have been destroyed during the process of concentration.
#Text=Milk
#Text=may also be desiccated; in this condition it is difficult of digestion.
#Text=In addition to the household methods of preserving fruits, large
#Text=quantities of fruits—both moist and dry—are now imported, protected
#Text=by syrup or sugar, in sealed canisters; and they retain the original
#Text=flavour almost unchanged.
#Text=The preservation of meat is effected by—
#Text=
#Text=1.
#Text==Drying.
#Text==—This must be done rapidly.
#Text=It is a process which is best
#Text=applicable to fish, but has been applied also to beef.
#Text=Dried Hamburg
#Text=beef is used for making sausages.
#Text=Pemmican, largely used by Arctic
#Text=voyagers, consists of a mixture of meat and fat, dried and powdered
#Text=along with some spices; it is generally eaten with some kind of meal.
#Text=
#Text=2.
#Text==Cold.
#Text==—Frozen meat now forms a very large part of the food of
#Text=the English people.
#Text=If the meat has been frozen before rigor mortis
#Text=(rigidity after death) has commenced, it keeps well; if frozen later,
#Text=it rapidly decomposes after being thawed.
#Text=Freezing arrests putrefaction
#Text=and tends to conceal its odour.
#Text=Hence the bad condition of frozen fish
#Text=may not be detected until it is cooked.
#Text=In cooking frozen meat, time
#Text=should be allowed for thawing to occur, before the meat is placed in
#Text=the oven.
#Text=Much of the ill-founded prejudice against frozen meat arises
#Text=from inattention to this point.
#Text=Frozen meat is equal in nutritive value
#Text=to and does not lose more in cooking than fresh meat.
#Text=
#Text=3.
#Text==Salting= may be done with brine or saltpetre (nitrate of
#Text=potassium); the latter does not decolourize the meat like the former.
#Text=Salted meats have lost much of their nutritive material, in the
#Text=form of albumin and salts, and the remaining meat is harder and more
#Text=difficult of digestion than fresh meat.
#Text=
#Text=4.
#Text==Immersion in antiseptic liquids= or gases, as sulphite of soda,
#Text=is objectionable, on account of the addition of extraneous, and not
#Text=altogether innocuous, salts.
#Text=Boric acid powder is largely used for
#Text=sprinkling on meat, particularly rabbits, etc., and for preserving hams
#Text=and other meats.
#Text=Its use is to be deprecated.
#Text=All such meats should be
#Text=thoroughly washed with water, before being cooked.
#Text=Solutions of _boric acid and borax_ are frequently added to milk.
#Text=Their
#Text=use is objectionable (_a_) because they tend to conceal incipient
#Text=decomposition, but do not prevent its possible evil effects, and (_b_)
#Text=because they enable the farmer to palm off dirty milk on the public.
#Text=Were the addition of preservatives to milk forbidden, the farmer could
#Text=perfectly well keep his milk sweet until it reached the town-consumer
#Text=by adopting strict measures of cleanliness, and by cooling his milk
#Text=before it leaves the farm.
#Text=At the least it should be made obligatory on
#Text=the milk retailer to declare the presence of preservatives in milk sold
#Text=by him.
#Text=The presence of borax or boric acid can be detected by evaporating
#Text= the milk to dryness, incinerating and then moistening the ash with a
#Text= drop of strong sulphuric acid.
#Text=If a little alcohol be now added, on
#Text= applying a light, a green flame indicates boric acid.
#Text=Milk or cream
#Text= containing boric acid turns blue litmus paper red.
#Text=_Formalin_ is also sometimes used as a preservative for milk in very
#Text=weak solution.
#Text=Its presence can be determined by diluting the milk with water in a
#Text= test-tube, and running strong sulphuric acid down the side of the
#Text= tube, taking care to prevent mixing.
#Text=At the junction of the acid and
#Text= diluted milk a violet ring is seen if formalin is present.
#Text=_Salicylic acid_ was formerly used as a milk preservative, but is now
#Text=seldom used except in beers.
#Text=All these preservatives are objectionable
#Text=in milk, although their injurious action may be difficult to prove.
#Text=
#Text=5.
#Text==Coating with fat or gelatine= has only succeeded in conjunction
#Text=with the exclusion of air.
#Text=This process is especially applicable to
#Text=fishes, as tinned sardines.
#Text=In a modified form, it is useful in coating
#Text=potted meats, etc.
#Text=
#Text=6.
#Text==Heating and Air-tight Cases.
#Text==—Tinned meats prepared according
#Text=to this method are imported in large quantities.
#Text=In the process of
#Text=preparation, the cases are packed with meat and filled up with gravy,
#Text=and then closed with a cover which is hermetically sealed, except at
#Text=one point.
#Text=The case is then heated to 250° Fahr., in order to drive
#Text=out all air, and destroy any putrefactive germs present.
#Text=The open
#Text=point is sealed while the gravy is still boiling, thus making the case
#Text=completely air-tight.
#Text=Albumin is coagulated at about 170° Fahr.; the
#Text=higher temperature, which it is found necessary to employ, overcooks
#Text=the meat and renders it less digestible (see also p. 40).
